title: Turmeric and honey cold-process soap
subtitle: A warm-coloured bar with turmeric as a natural colourant and honey for conditioning
excerpt: A cold-process bar soap with turmeric powder for a golden colour and honey stirred in at trace for conditioning. The oil base is 55% olive, 30% coconut, 15% castor. Allow 4-6 weeks to harden before use.
sourceNotes: Susan Miller Cavitch, The Soapmaker's Companion (1997): honey and natural colourant additives in cold-process soap; heat management for sugar additives. SoapCalc (accessed May 2026): SAP values for this oil blend. Wikipedia on curcumin pigment stability in alkaline environments.

content.text: Turmeric and honey are two of the oldest soap additives in the tradition. Turmeric gives a warm golden colour through its curcumin pigment. The colour shifts a little in cold-process soap and lands somewhere between amber and warm ochre. Honey adds natural sugars and softens the bar. It also brings a very faint warmth to the lather.
Both honey and turmeric affect the batter's behaviour. Honey is a sugar, and sugars accelerate
saponification
slightly, which can push
trace

faster than expected. Both additives go in at light trace, not before. Keep the oils and lye solution at the lower end of the temperature range (38°C rather than 45°C) and add them promptly once light trace is confirmed.
Honey also intensifies
gel phase

. Use light insulation rather than a thick blanket, and check the mould at 2 and 4 hours. If the centre feels very hot, remove the insulation. An overheated honey soap can crack or develop a hollow centre. Wear protective gloves and goggles throughout the lye handling.
What you need

items.name: Castor oil (cosmetic)
items.prepNote: 15%. Higher castor than usual for a more stable, creamy lather that flatters the honey-conditioning effect.

items.name: Ground turmeric
items.prepNote: 1% of oil weight. Dispersed into a tablespoon of the weighed olive oil before adding to the batch, to avoid clumps. The colour ranges from warm yellow at 0.5% to deep amber-ochre at 1.5%; start low.

items.name: Honey
items.prepNote: 3% of oil weight. Add at light trace, stirred in by hand. Slightly warmed honey (30°C) disperses more evenly than cold, solid honey.

content.text: Method
Prepare the additives

Weigh the turmeric powder and mix it into one tablespoon of the weighed olive oil. Stir until a smooth paste forms. Set aside. Weigh the honey into a small dish and warm it gently if it is very thick.
Make the lye solution and prepare the oils

Gloves and goggles on. Add the sodium hydroxide to the distilled water and stir until dissolved. Allow to cool to 38°C. Melt the coconut oil if solid, combine with the remaining olive oil, castor oil, and the turmeric-oil paste. Stir until evenly coloured. Allow the oil blend to reach 38°C.
Blend and add honey at trace
Pour the lye solution into the oils slowly. Stick-blend in short bursts. At light
trace
, add the honey and stir in by hand. Work quickly; the sugars will begin to accelerate thickening immediately. Pour into the mould as soon as the honey is evenly distributed.
Insulate and monitor
Cover with cardboard only, not a thick blanket. Check the mould at 2 hours and again at 4 hours. If the centre is very warm to the touch, remove the cover entirely. The
superfat
oils and honey will carry enough heat for gel phase without extra insulation. Leave 24-48 hours before unmoulding. Wear gloves to unmould and cut. Cure on an open rack for 4-6 weeks.
Storage and shelf life

Keep cured bars on an open rack in a cool, dark place. The turmeric colour fades gradually over months without indicating spoilage; this is normal. The bar keeps 2 years from cure date. Discard on DOS (orange spots on the cut face) or a sharp, paint-like smell.
